of the plugin that caused the failure.

For errors that involve two plugins (e.g. a duplicate export
format id) this field holds the second plugin's id, and both
ids appear in the error message.

Description of a single component in a shape an LLM can reason
about — name, purpose, props, and an optional concrete example.

An array of these is passed to the LLM so it knows what components
are available and how to use them. Produced by
; Core only owns the destination shape.
The component name as registered in the Puck config (e.g.

Human-readable description of what the component does and when
to use it. Forwarded verbatim into the LLM prompt — write it
as guidance to a collaborator.

Optional concrete example of a valid prop bag. The LLM uses
this as a one-shot example of how the fields should look when
populated; leaving it off falls back to zero-shot from the
field descriptions alone.

Description of a single prop on a component, in a shape an LLM can
reason about.

(Phase 3) produces an
array of these from a Puck

field descriptor. The AI
copilot plugin then feeds them into its system prompt so the model
knows which props it can set and what shape each one takes.

the compiler enforce that
, etc. That refactor is **breaking** for the

AI
contract, so it is deferred to the next contract major; until then,

own the per-type invariants
at runtime (Zod).

content.text:
An unsubscribe function. Calling it is idempotent and
detaches the handler immediately.

summary.text: The root IR document — the single argument passed to every

(Phase 3). Core
declares the shape; it does not implement the transformation.

Top-level asset manifest. Every asset referenced anywhere in
the tree should appear here exactly once; node-scoped

entries are an optimization for
exporters that process nodes independently.

The root node of the page tree.

Exporters walk this recursively to produce output.

Schema version of this IR document.

Literal
for the initial contract. A future

would be
a breaking change to IR shape and requires a migration shim in

. String (not number) to avoid JSON
floating-point surprises and to match the convention used by
most serialized IR schemas.

An out-of-band resource referenced by the page — images, videos,
fonts, stylesheets, inline scripts, etc.

Assets are listed both at the root of

(so exporters
can emit a manifest or preload block in one pass) and, optionally,
on individual

s (so a node-scoped exporter can
resolve its own dependencies without walking the whole tree).

The same asset may appear in both places — exporters are expected
to deduplicate by

Stable, unique identifier for this asset within the IR.

Typically a hash of the URL or a caller-provided slug. Exporters
use this for deduplication when the same asset is referenced by
multiple nodes.

The asset's category.

Closed union — extending this list is a breaking change to the
IR contract. Use

for anything that doesn't fit the
named categories rather than introducing an ad-hoc string.

Free-form metadata the producer wants to pass through to
consumers (e.g.

for an image).

Exporters should treat unknown keys as forward-compatible — a
missing key must not cause a hard failure.

Absolute or root-relative URL pointing at the asset.

Exporters may rewrite this (e.g. to a CDN or a hashed filename)
during serialization — the IR shape does not constrain the
scheme.

Page-level metadata — title, description, and timestamps the
exporter may surface in

, a feed, or a manifest.

All fields are optional so minimal IR snapshots can be produced
without forcing the caller to synthesize values they don't have.

Timestamps are ISO 8601 strings (not

instances) so the IR
remains JSON-serializable without a custom replacer.
Optional ISO 8601 timestamp of page creation.
Optional ISO 8601 timestamp of most-recent page update.

A single node in the page IR tree.

Structurally recursive: every node may carry a

array of
further nodes, mirroring the component tree the author laid out in
the editor. Leaf nodes omit

entirely rather than setting
it to an empty array (the distinction is not semantically
meaningful, but omitting the key keeps serialized IR snapshots
smaller).

instance. Used by exporters for

, the editor SHOULD treat this node and its subtree

config.

Plugin types are deliberately **not** propagated into

(core decouples its type version from every plugin's — see the
"Plugin config & type safety" section in the plugin-authoring
guide). The runtime still validates

It is intentionally empty by default and intersected (not replaced)
with the open

, so this stays purely
additive — no existing config breaks, and unaugmented keys keep
working untyped.
Header action descriptor, contributed by plugins via

.

Plugins author header actions as **plain data** — no React, no
components, no closures over render-time state. The Studio shell
(

to the live React tree at runtime.

Keeping the type free of

layer stay React-free (architecture §17) and lets test
code compare action arrays with structural equality.

The full shape was finalized in

can reference it without crossing the
types → runtime boundary;

re-exports it
for callers that import from

Optional predicate that disables the action based on live
context. Called on every render of the header (cheap by
convention — do not perform async work or expensive
computation here).

Receives the same
Display group the action belongs to. Used by

— normal toolbar action (e.g. "Save Draft").
  **Default** when omitted — most actions belong here.
-

— collapsed into the "…" menu when space is
  tight (e.g. "Generate with AI", "Download HTML").

layer
cannot import React, and plugin-contributed action arrays must
stay serializable enough that lifecycle tests can compare them
with

. The string-name indirection lets the renderer
own React while the protocol stays headless.

**Resolvable set.** The shell resolves against a *curated*
registry (not all of

) so the chrome bundle
tree-shakes to only the icons it ships — a namespace import would
retain every Lucide icon. Name matching is case/separator
insensitive (

all
resolve). A name outside the registry resolves to no icon (the
label still renders). To add an icon, extend

(and the chrome-path
bundle-budget gate covers the size impact).

Stable, globally-unique identifier for the action
(e.g.

rejects duplicate ids across all contributing plugins.

Convention: lowercase, hyphen-separated, no namespace prefix —
the runtime treats ids as opaque strings.

Human-readable label rendered inside the button (or shown in
the overflow menu) by the Studio shell.

Required so plugin authors cannot ship a button with no visible
affordance. Localization is the host app's responsibility — the
runtime treats this as an opaque display string.

Click handler invoked by the Studio shell when the user
activates the action. Receives the same

the lifecycle hooks receive, so
the action can read live page data, call

events
to other plugins.

The runtime awaits the returned promise (if any) before
re-enabling the button — host apps are free to render a
loading affordance during the wait.

Errors thrown from

are caught by the shell and routed
through
; they do not crash the editor.

Per-group ordering hint. Lower values render first; the default
is

so plugin authors can interleave their actions with
built-ins (which conventionally use round numbers like

).

### Capability gating

Every callback below is **optional**. The sidebar uses capability
detection — "is the callback defined?" — to decide whether to render
the matching affordance. A host that implements

gets the
inline rename input; a host that doesn't gets a plain row. The
existing

gating is the precedent.

### Data-flow rule (PRD §3.3)

**No optimistic mutation in core.** Every callback resolves → host
mutates its registry → host invokes the

: it may return the created page so the UI can
pre-select it before the refresh round-trips.

the page and (optionally) navigates to it. Async result is
awaited by the dialog so it can show a pending state.

when this callback is undefined OR when

Duplicate a page. Hidden when undefined.

resolve with the created

so the UI can pre-
select it before the standard

appears once the host's

when this callback is undefined OR when

callback is undefined.

### Read access

-

returns a snapshot of the current Puck page data.
  Cheap to call; no subscription is created.
-

is the frozen, merged Studio configuration.

### Write access

- All mutations go through

per architecture §12. Plugins have **no** direct Zustand
  store access — that boundary prevents two plugins from fighting
  over the same store key and ensures every mutation is captured in
  Puck's history stack.

### Observability

-

broadcasts to the plugin event bus so other plugins
  can observe this one (subscribers wire up their own listeners in
— Core does not prescribe a subscription API).

Broadcast an event to the Studio plugin event bus.

Event names are free-form strings — Core does not enforce a
schema. Subscribers validate payloads themselves.

content.text:
**Not implemented yet (architecture §12).** The
plugin-to-plugin bus has no delivery: calling

is inert,
never throws, and no subscriber receives the event. The

shell logs a warning on the first call per plugin
context (every environment — rate-limited to once) so the inert
contract is discoverable without reading the implementation. Do
not rely on delivery until a concrete subscribe API and ordering
semantics are documented here. The signature is stable; only the
runtime behavior changes when the bus ships.

Every hook is optional. The lifecycle manager (

### Ordering

Hooks fire in plugin registration order, which mirrors the order of
the host app's

to abort the
  publish. The host receives the error and can surface it.
- All other hooks' errors are logged and swallowed — a buggy plugin
  must not crash the editor.

### Statelessness

Hooks receive a fresh

pointer at every
call site, so plugins can stay stateless and read the current data
/ config at the moment the hook fires.

post-publish side-effects like cache invalidation or telemetry.

This constant is the reference value

for the range check.

### Why hand-maintained instead of generated?

Rslib's bundleless build preserves a 1:1

mapping, and
wiring a codegen step to stamp

's version into this
file would add build-order complexity for a single string. The test
in

is the forcing function that keeps the two in
sync, which is simpler and equally robust.

Create a lifecycle manager bound to a frozen array of plugin
registrations.

The registrations are snapshotted at construction time — adding
or removing plugins at runtime would require rebuilding the
manager, which mirrors Puck's mount-time plugin array semantics.

### Layer map

The named exports below are grouped by the four M2–M5 layers:

- **Types** — the pure TypeScript types plugin authors and host apps
  use to describe plugins, registrations, config, and domain records.
  Re-exported via

so the barrel stays free of runtime
  type imports.
- **Runtime** — the React-free plugin engine:

, the
  error class family, the export registry + header-action composer,
  and the lifecycle manager factory. React never appears in this
  layer; the

quality gate enforces
  that at CI time.
- **Config** — the Zod schema, the layered-merge factory, the React
  provider, and the

helper, and the three Zustand store hooks
  (

compat adapter
— is **intentionally absent** from this barrel. It is reachable only
at the

subpath so ESM tree-shaking can drop
it entirely from host bundles that never import it. The

script asserts the adapter is absent from
a build that imports only

acceptance criterion #6.

Internal primitives that are not part of the public contract — e.g.

internals, internal schema
helpers — are deliberately not surfaced here. Consumers who need a
deeper hook reach for the

summary.text:
by layering schema
defaults, env vars, and explicit host overrides — in that order.

### Layering

1. **Layer 1 — defaults.**

produces
   the fully-populated default shape. This is the base target for

, so missing keys in higher layers fall back
   to real values rather than

argument
   is treated as an opaque

and layered last, so it
   beats both defaults and env.

After merging, the result is re-parsed through the schema. This
re-parse is load-bearing: it re-runs every validator (URL format,
integer bounds, enum membership) against the merged shape and
catches env-induced type mismatches that layers 1 and 3 would
have passed cleanly on their own.

### Errors

Any
raised during the final parse is wrapped in a

whose message lists every failing field
in

so host apps that need structured access can drill in
with

Pure type-level cast — the runtime value is the same object the caller
passed in. Exists because the brand property is required on the
sub-interface (to prevent inference pollution) but has no runtime
representation; this helper concentrates the unavoidable

cast in
one place so plugin authors don't sprinkle it across factories.

shape.

Puck's plugin type is a bag of optional fields (

) — there is no single required field to pin
the check on. This guard looks for **any** of the distinctive Puck
contribution surfaces:

-

— a plugin-provided side panel or toolbar.

An object that contributes none of those is either a vacuous
plugin (which callers should author as a

instead)
or something that was handed to the runtime by mistake. Rejecting
the empty object here means

instead of silently registering a no-op.

Crucially, this guard **also** rejects anything that looks like a
— the two guards are mutually exclusive so

branch.

Pure, synchronous, no side effects.

shape.

The check is intentionally minimal — it validates the three fields
the runtime actually reads during compilation:

1.

is a function.

Everything else on

(name, version,
description) is advisory and not required for the runtime to make
forward progress. A plugin that passes this guard but omits those
fields will still compile; the type system is the forcing function
for completeness at authoring time.

Pure, synchronous, no side effects.
Wrap an async plugin loader as a
. The actual plugin module is fetched on first

Compose a list of plugin-contributed Puck override slices into a
single

dictionary, with per-key currying so
multiple plugins touching the same key all run.

The input array is consumed in order: earlier entries become
**innermost** wrappers (closer to the default render), later
entries become **outermost**. This matches Puck 0.21's own
internal composition so feeding the result back into

is semantically identical to letting Puck
register each plugin individually — just done once, upfront, so

can also fold in consumer-supplied overrides in the
same pass.
